one_line_takeaway: TamperBench is a unified, hyperparameter-swept benchmark showing that all 21 tested open-weight LLMs -- regardless of family, scale, or alignment-stage defense -- can be tampered via fine-tuning or representation attacks to reach StrongREJECT harmfulness above 0.74 while keeping utility loss under 10%, with competing-objectives jailbreak-tuning the most consistently effective attack and current alignment-stage defenses largely failing to close the gap.
key_finding: For all 21 evaluated open-weight LLMs, the worst-case post-attack StrongREJECT harmfulness score (SRmax) exceeds 0.74, i.e., every model tested admits at least one tampering attack that drives harmfulness far above baseline while keeping MMLU-Pro degradation under 10%. Competing-objectives jailbreak-tuning is the most severe attack overall, achieving the highest StrongREJECT score in 14 of 21 models (mean StrongREJECT = 0.84), ahead of full-parameter fine-tuning (0.82), LoRA fine-tuning (0.81), backdoor jailbreak-tuning (0.79), multilingual fine-tuning (0.76), and style-modulation (0.76); the inference-time embedding attack produced comparatively mild harmfulness increases for 7-8B models. Within the Qwen3 family post-trained variants were generally more tamper-resistant on average than base variants (e.g., Qwen3-4B SRmal-avg = 0.76 vs. 0.84 for Qwen3-4B-Base; SRmax = 0.85 vs. 0.89), whereas the Llama-3 family showed the opposite direction (instruction-tuned Llama-3 variants reached higher post-tampering harmfulness than base counterparts, especially at 1B scale), which the authors attribute to differing rubric sub-components (refusal-rate differences vs. response-quality differences) rather than a single universal mechanism. Mistral-7B-Instruct had a markedly higher untampered baseline StrongREJECT (0.64) than Mistral-7B-Base (0.31), attributed in manual analysis to the base model's poor instruction-following rather than a true safety difference, and also reached among the highest post-tampering scores (SRmax = 0.90). Among the five publicly released defense-augmented Llama-3-8B-Instruct variants, CRL and TAR showed partial improvement (CRL: SRben-avg 0.26 vs. 0.35 undefended, but SRmal-avg 0.85 vs. 0.85 and SRmax 0.87 vs. 0.88, essentially matching undefended under malicious attacks; TAR: SRmax reduced by 0.12 but at the cost of untampered MMLU-Pro dropping to ~0.18 vs. ~0.45 for CRL/undefended). Among the seven re-implemented alignment-stage defenses tested on Llama-3-8B/-Instruct and Qwen3-8B, CTRL and T-Vaccine cratered capability on Llama (MMLU-Pro dropping to 0.07-0.09 from 0.38-0.46) while harmfulness still largely persisted (SRmal-avg ≥0.70); Booster, CRL, RSN-Tune, and SDD all left SRmal-avg within 0.04 of the undefended baseline. Preliminary scaling experiments show larger models remain similarly tamperable: under harmful LoRA, Qwen3-32B reached StrongREJECT 0.86 (vs. 0.81 for Qwen3-8B) and Llama-3-70B-Instruct reached 0.93 (vs. 0.83 for Llama-3-8B-Instruct); under competing-objectives jailbreak-tuning Qwen3-32B reached 0.88 (vs. 0.89 for Qwen3-8B). Relaxing the utility constraint from 10% to 20% MMLU-Pro drop further increased measured harmfulness (notably for Qwen3-0.6B and Qwen3-4B), and removing the constraint entirely produced misleadingly high harmfulness scores paired with near-total capability collapse (e.g., Qwen3-8B full fine-tuning: MMLU-Pro 59% to 18%), which the authors cite as justification for their utility-constrained attack-success definition.
core_contribution: The paper introduces TamperBench, described as the first unified open-source benchmark and toolkit for systematically evaluating tamper resistance of open-weight LLMs. It (1) consolidates a repository of weight-space fine-tuning attacks (benign/harmful full fine-tuning, LoRA, multilingual fine-tuning, backdoor/style-modulation/competing-objectives jailbreak-tuning) and a latent-space embedding attack, plus seven re-implemented alignment-stage defenses (Booster, CRL, CTRL, RSN-Tune, SDD, T-Vaccine, TAR), into a single extensible Python framework built on HuggingFace/vLLM/Optuna; (2) performs systematic per-attack-per-model Optuna hyperparameter sweeps (40 trials) to find worst-case attack configurations rather than relying on fixed hyperparameters as prior work does; and (3) reports a large comparative evaluation of 21 open-weight LLMs (Qwen3, Llama-3/3.2, Mistral-7B families, plus 5 publicly released defense-augmented Llama-3-8B-Instruct variants: ReFAT, RR, CRL, TAR, LAT) across nine tampering attacks, using StrongREJECT for harmfulness and MMLU-Pro for utility, with an explicit utility constraint (≤10% MMLU-Pro drop) used to define a 'successful' attack. The paper is presented as an author's extended version of a KDD'26 paper; an earlier version appeared at the AIA workshop as 'SafeTuneBed.'
threat_model: White-box, worst-case open-weight access: the attacker has full access to model weights and internal representations and can apply any weight-space modification (full fine-tuning, LoRA, backdoor/style/competing-objectives jailbreak-tuning, multilingual fine-tuning) or representation-space perturbation (embedding attack) at will. Two attacker intents are modeled: accidental/non-adversarial tampering (benign fine-tuning for a downstream task that inadvertently erodes safety) and malicious tampering (intentional harmful fine-tuning or representation manipulation to elicit unsafe outputs). A 'successful' tamper is defined as one that both raises StrongREJECT harmfulness and keeps utility degradation to ≤10% relative MMLU-Pro drop (to rule out attacks that merely destroy general capability rather than genuinely removing safety guardrails). Defenders are alignment-stage methods that must harden a base model before release, since the paper argues fine-tuning-stage and post-tuning-stage defenses presuppose centralized control unavailable for redistributed open-weight models.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the benchmark studies only refusal-based defenses against harmful behavior and does not cover ignorance-based (unlearning/knowledge-removal) defense approaches; (2) the core sweep evaluation (Section 4.1-4.3) is restricted to the 0.6-8B parameter regime, with only preliminary, reduced-sweep (30-trial, limited attack subset) experiments extending to 32B/70B models, so broader large-model coverage remains future work. Additional caveats surfaced in the paper's own text (not authors' formal Limitations section but methodologically relevant): the utility-constrained attack-success definition is a design choice the authors motivate but acknowledge 'may not be a general requirement for a successful attack'; StrongREJECT is used as the single representative safety metric for the main experiments (other benchmarks like JailbreakBench/Policy-Eval/SafetyGap are only used for correlation validation, not full reporting); MMLU-Pro utility is evaluated on a reduced 560-sample subset for computational efficiency rather than the full benchmark; the five publicly released defense-augmented Llama-3-8B-Instruct checkpoints (ReFAT, RR, CRL, TAR, LAT) were used as-is from original authors rather than retrained within TamperBench, differing methodologically from the seven re-implemented defenses trained from scratch under default hyperparameters.

datasets_used: MMLU-Pro (560-sample subset used as primary utility metric)
JailbreakBench, Policy-Eval, SafetyGap (used only for cross-metric correlation validation)
IFEval, MT-Bench, Minerva-Math, LAB-Bench, LiveBench-Coding (supported capability benchmarks; used for correlation validation, not primary reporting)
LAT-Harmful dataset (64 examples, used for harmful LoRA fine-tuning attacks including the 32B/70B scaling experiments)
quantitative_results: Worst-case post-attack StrongREJECT (SRmax) exceeds 0.74 for all 21 evaluated models
Competing-objectives jailbreak-tuning: highest StrongREJECT in 14/21 models, mean SR = 0.84
Full-parameter fine-tuning mean SR = 0.82; LoRA fine-tuning mean SR = 0.81; backdoor jailbreak-tuning 0.79; multilingual 0.76; style-modulation 0.76
Qwen3-4B vs Qwen3-4B-Base: SRmal-avg 0.76 vs 0.84; SRmax 0.85 vs 0.89
Mistral-7B-Instruct untampered StrongREJECT 0.64 vs Mistral-7B-Base 0.31; Mistral-7B-Instruct SRmax = 0.90
CRL (Llama-3-8B-Instruct defense variant): SRben-avg 0.26 vs 0.35 undefended; SRmal-avg 0.85 vs 0.85 undefended; SRmax 0.87 vs 0.88 undefended

TAR (Llama-3-8B-Instruct defense variant): reduces SRmax by 0.12 but untampered MMLU-Pro drops to ~0.18 vs ~0.45 for CRL/undefended (8 points of that drop attributed to instruction-following format errors)
CTRL and T-Vaccine (re-implemented defenses on Llama): MMLU-Pro drops to 0.07-0.09 from 0.38-0.46, yet SRmal-avg remains ≥0.70
Booster, CRL, RSN-Tune, SDD (re-implemented defenses): SRmal-avg within 0.04 of undefended baseline
Qwen3-32B under harmful LoRA: StrongREJECT 0.86 (vs 0.81 for Qwen3-8B); Llama-3-70B-Instruct: 0.93 (vs 0.83 for Llama-3-8B-Instruct)
Qwen3-32B under competing-objectives jailbreak-tuning: 0.88 (vs 0.89 for Qwen3-8B)
Unconstrained harmfulness maximization example: Qwen3-8B full fine-tuning MMLU-Pro drops from 59% to 18%
Correlation of StrongREJECT increases with JailbreakBench ρ=0.71, Policy-Eval ρ=0.54, SafetyGap ρ=0.66
MMLU-Pro ≥10% drop co-occurs with ≥10% drop on IFEval/MT-Bench/Minerva-Math/LiveBench-Coding in 79-89% of cases

40-trial Optuna sweep per model-attack pair for main 21-model evaluation; 30-trial sweeps for 32B/70B scaling experiments; 21 defended checkpoints evaluated across 7 re-implemented defenses x 3 base models
baselines_compared: 21 open-weight LLMs: Qwen3-0.6B/1.7B/4B/8B (base and instruct variants), Llama-3.2-1B/3B (base and instruct), Llama-3-8B (base and instruct), Mistral-7B (base and instruct)
5 publicly released defense-augmented Llama-3-8B-Instruct checkpoints: ReFAT, Representation Routing/Circuit Breakers (RR), CRL, TAR, LAT
7 re-implemented alignment-stage defenses trained from default hyperparameters: Booster, CRL, CTRL, RSN-Tune, SDD, T-Vaccine, TAR

9 tampering attacks: benign full fine-tuning, benign LoRA, harmful full fine-tuning, harmful LoRA, multilingual fine-tuning, backdoor jailbreak-tuning, style-modulation jailbreak-tuning, competing-objectives jailbreak-tuning, embedding (representation-space) attack

Prior tamper-resistance evaluation frameworks discussed as related work but not directly benchmarked against: HarmBench (prompt-based red-teaming only, no weight-space tampering), and unnamed prior toolkits [45,54,63] criticized for limited extensibility and attack coverage
